claims.evidence_text: The median productivity of wheat increases slightly by +2.5% (5th , 95th percentiles: -15.2%, +53.5%) under NT R.
claims.claim_summary: Simulated global median wheat productivity increased by +2.5% under no-tillage with residue retention (NT R).

claims.evidence_text: wheat productivity increases substantially under NT R whereas this effect diminishes with increases in aridity indexes (Fig. 3.2A).
claims.claim_summary: Rain-fed wheat productivity gains under no-tillage with residue are largest in arid regions and diminish as aridity decreases.

claims.evidence_text: In 1995, wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) was planted after soybean, which resulted in a maize-soybean wheat rotation.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat was grown in a maize-soybean-wheat crop rotation (wheat following soybean).

claims.evidence_text: During the experiment, the site was cropped with a maize-wheat rotation, with maize being sown in April, harvested in October and directly followed by tillage (20 cm for tillage treatments) and sowing
claims.claim_summary: Wheat was grown in a maize-wheat rotation, sown directly after maize harvest in October.

claims.evidence_text: During the experiment, the maize received N-fertilizers in May and wheat in February and April (Table 4.2).
claims.claim_summary: Wheat received nitrogen fertilizer in February and April (split spring application).
